mainText:
If you only remember one thing today, make it this: Mira Murati's company, Thinking Machines, released Inkling. It is a huge multimodal model with open weights, and it is instantly the strongest American open model. Open source A I just got a new U S flagbearer.

Section one. Inkling. This comes from your A I News by swyx, and your Axios A I plus save in Readwise. Thinking Machines launched Inkling, a nine hundred seventy five billion parameter multimodal model, with forty one billion active parameters. There is also a smaller version called Inkling Small. Both are open weights under a permissive license. Swyx calls it the new best American open model. Your Axios save adds the business angle. Inkling is built for customization. Companies can tune it to their own needs. The pitch is flexibility and lower cost, not raw power. Why it matters: a frontier class open model from a U S lab resets the balance between open and closed A I, and gives businesses a real alternative to A P I only labs.

Section two. The model race. Google reportedly scrapped the base architecture of Gemini three point five Pro after enterprise testing found failures in tool calling and math reasoning. They rebuilt it from scratch, and the launch is targeted for tomorrow, July seventeenth. Meanwhile, your Rundown newsletter covers Open A I's hardware debut, a device that controls Codex, its coding agent. And chip maker T S M C reports full second quarter earnings tomorrow, after confirming record revenue of about thirty nine point six billion dollars, up thirty six percent from last year. Why it matters: tomorrow is a real inflection day. Gemini's comeback and T S M C's outlook will show whether the A I boom keeps its pace.

Section three. A I policy. Politico reports the deepest regulatory split yet. Anthropic is endorsing state A I safety laws, including California S B fifty three, New York's Raise Act, and bills in Illinois and Massachusetts. Open A I is fighting for one federal standard that would override state rules. Both companies are heading toward I P Os later this year, so regulatory strategy is now an investor story too. In Europe, the A I Act has entered its enforcement phase. Chatbot disclosure rules are live, and from August second, the European Commission can fine general purpose A I providers up to fifteen million euros, or three percent of global revenue. Also from your Axios A I plus in Readwise: Anthropic is hiring experts to prevent A I misuse for weapons, and a new report flags Google Search's A I features as risky for children. Why it matters: the rules of A I are being written right now, and the two biggest labs are betting on opposite playbooks.

Section four. Chips and geopolitics. U S export controls keep tightening. Commerce Department guidance restricts Nvidia's advanced A I chips, including the Blackwell series, from going to China, and closes the loophole of buying through overseas subsidiaries. Nvidia's own filings say the company is effectively shut out of China's data center market. Watch T S M C's guidance tomorrow for any sign that A I chip demand is cooling. Why it matters: chip policy is now the main lever of tech geopolitics, and the first place any A I slowdown would show.

Section five. Media watch, from your Status saves in Readwise. Natalie Korach covers M S Now's pivotal moment, the New York Times' standoff over a subpoena, and Musk's deepfakes lawsuit. Oliver Darcy reports that C N N chief Mark Thompson quietly visited the White House. And from The Comms Stack in your Reads: an argument that A I native comms teams should hire more juniors, not fewer, plus tips on avoiding A I slop in thought leadership. Why it matters: press freedom fights and A I reshaped newsrooms are converging, and that is squarely your world.

Section six. Tech for good, and the good news close. The A I for Good Summit in Geneva showed brain computer interfaces restoring communication, A I powered robotic wheelchairs, and hand exoskeletons driven by muscle signals. A project called Diverssity won the women entrepreneurs prize for A I mental health support for neurodiverse teenagers. On accountability, your Noosphere newsletter covers Brazilian prosecutors suing Axia Energia over the Parakana health crisis, where residents say, we are drinking mud. Why it matters: the same technology making headlines for power struggles is quietly restoring mobility and speech. That's the top of the news. Your full briefing is in the recording.
